text:
The Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes (TALSPEAK) process was pioneered at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in the 1960s by Weaver and Kappelmann. Originally developed as an alternative to the TRAMEX process, TALSPEAK functions by selectively extracting trivalent lanthanides into an organic phase while retaining trivalent actinides in the aqueous phase via aminopolycarboxylate complexation. Over decades of refinement, the conventional TALSPEAK system—utilizing di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (HDEHP) in 1,4-di-isopropylbenzene and a concentrated lactate buffer containing diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)—has established itself as the benchmark for the aqueous partitioning of 4f and 5f elements. This chemistry has also been successfully adapted for actinide-selective stripping in "Reverse TALSPEAK" configurations. 

While the process can achieve impressive single-stage separation factors of approximately 100 for the challenging Am³⁺/Nd³⁺ pair, its industrial adoption remains limited by two primary technical hurdles: the requirement for stringent pH control within a narrow window and relatively sluggish phase-transfer kinetics. Consequently, high concentrations of carboxylic acid buffers (0.5–2 M lactate) are currently necessary to maintain performance. These complexities have catalyzed a decade of intensive research aimed at unraveling the fundamental chemical interactions governing TALSPEAK and related aqueous holdback systems. This review examines recent progress in understanding the underlying mechanisms of these processes and discusses how these insights are shaping the next generation of actinide-lanthanide separations.
